description: Review readiness for an information security management system built around risk management, governance, controls, audit, and continual improvement.

questions.question: Has the ISMS scope been documented with boundaries, locations, assets, technologies, and interested parties?
questions.description: The scope should explain what is included, what is excluded, and why exclusions are justified.
questions.recommendation: Document the ISMS scope and validate it against business processes, assets, third parties, and regulatory expectations.

questions.question: Is an information security policy approved by leadership and communicated to relevant personnel?
questions.description: The policy should support business objectives and set the direction for the ISMS.
questions.recommendation: Obtain leadership approval for the security policy and publish it through onboarding, intranet, or policy tooling.

questions.question: Are ISMS roles, responsibilities, and authorities assigned and understood?
questions.description: Include ISMS owner, risk owners, control owners, internal audit owner, and incident roles.
questions.recommendation: Create an ISMS responsibility matrix and review ownership during management review meetings.

questions.question: Is a repeatable information security risk assessment methodology defined and used?
questions.description: The method should define likelihood, impact, risk acceptance criteria, owners, and review frequency.
questions.recommendation: Formalize the risk methodology and apply it consistently across assets, suppliers, systems, and processes.

questions.question: Are risk treatment plans approved, tracked, and linked to selected controls?
questions.description: Treatment should address avoid, reduce, transfer, or accept decisions with accountable owners and target dates.
questions.recommendation: Maintain risk treatment plans with owners, due dates, control mapping, and evidence of acceptance.

questions.question: Is the Statement of Applicability maintained with justification for included and excluded Annex A controls?
questions.description: The SoA should reflect control applicability, implementation status, and risk treatment decisions.
questions.recommendation: Update the Statement of Applicability after each risk assessment and management review cycle.

questions.question: Are measurable information security objectives defined, monitored, and reported?
questions.description: Objectives can include risk reduction, training completion, vulnerability SLAs, incident response, and audit closure.
questions.recommendation: Define measurable ISMS objectives with targets, owners, data sources, and review cadence.

questions.question: Are competence, awareness, and training requirements defined for people affecting the ISMS?
questions.description: Include employees, contractors, privileged users, control owners, and incident responders.
questions.recommendation: Build a role-based training plan and retain completion evidence for all relevant personnel.

questions.question: Is documented information controlled for versioning, approvals, access, retention, and disposal?
questions.description: Policies, procedures, records, and audit evidence should be protected from unauthorized changes.
questions.recommendation: Use a controlled document repository with version history, approval workflow, and access restrictions.

questions.question: Is an asset inventory maintained with owners, classification, location, and lifecycle status?
questions.description: Assets should include hardware, software, cloud resources, data stores, SaaS platforms, and critical documents.
questions.recommendation: Create an asset inventory with owners and classification, then reconcile it with endpoint, cloud, and procurement data.

questions.question: Are identity and access management controls enforced for joiners, movers, leavers, and privileged accounts?
questions.description: Review least privilege, access approval, MFA, privileged access, and periodic access reviews.
questions.recommendation: Implement a documented access lifecycle with MFA, privileged account controls, and recurring certification.

questions.question: Are cryptographic controls defined for encryption, key management, and approved algorithms?
questions.description: The control should address data at rest, data in transit, secrets, certificates, rotation, and key access.
questions.recommendation: Publish a cryptographic standard and maintain evidence for key ownership, rotation, and access review.

questions.question: Are secure configuration, patching, malware protection, and vulnerability management operating on critical systems?
questions.description: Include servers, endpoints, cloud services, network devices, containers, and applications.
questions.recommendation: Define secure baselines, patch SLAs, vulnerability scanning, and exception handling for critical systems.

questions.question: Are logs collected, protected, reviewed, and used to detect information security events?
questions.description: Critical systems should send logs to a protected location with alerting and retention aligned to risk.
questions.recommendation: Centralize security logs, define alert use cases, protect log integrity, and review incidents regularly.

questions.question: Are supplier security risks assessed before onboarding and reviewed during the relationship?
questions.description: Consider due diligence, contractual controls, data access, monitoring, exit, and concentration risk.
questions.recommendation: Establish supplier risk tiers, security due diligence, contract clauses, and annual reviews for high-risk suppliers.

questions.question: Are physical, environmental, and cloud infrastructure safeguards aligned to asset criticality?
questions.description: Include office access, visitor controls, device protection, cloud account hardening, and administrative access.
questions.recommendation: Document physical and cloud infrastructure safeguards and verify them through periodic reviews.

questions.question: Are internal audits and management reviews performed at planned intervals?
questions.description: Audit and review outputs should include ISMS performance, risk status, objective results, incidents, and improvement actions.
questions.recommendation: Schedule internal audits and management reviews, then track actions to completion with evidence.

questions.question: Are nonconformities, corrective actions, and continual improvement items tracked to closure?
questions.description: Root cause analysis should prevent recurrence and improve the ISMS over time.
questions.recommendation: Maintain a corrective action register with root cause, owner, due date, verification, and closure evidence.
